d: The optimal production quantity in the last period with positive production is y_t = min{C_t, d_t + … + d_T}, meaning produce as much as possible up to the remaining demand or capacity limit.

b: Each order placed in the optimal solution covers exactly the demands of several consecutive periods, and once the order times are determined, the order quantities are fully determined.
c: An optimal solution exists if and only if the cumulative demand for every prefix of periods is met by at least half of the cumulative capacity up to that period.
d: The problem can be solved via a shortest path algorithm on a network where each arc (i, j) represents an uncapacitated production sequence with cost l_{ij} equal to the minimum cost of satisfying demands from period i+1 through j using such a sequence.
gen_text_clean:
question:
Consider the capacitated DELS model where production capacity constraints are added. Which of the following statements is true regarding an optimal solution?

a) The optimal policy can be represented as a series of production sequences, each covering multiple periods with at most one period producing less than its full capacity.

b) Each order placed in the optimal solution covers exactly the demands of several consecutive periods, and once the order times are determined, the order quantities are fully determined.

c) An optimal solution exists if and only if the cumulative demand for every prefix of periods is met by at least half of the cumulative capacity up to that period.

d) The problem can be solved via a shortest path algorithm on a network where each arc (i, j) represents an uncapacitated production sequence with cost l_{ij} equal to the minimum cost of satisfying demands from period i+1 through j using such a sequence.

correct_key: A
difficulty: hard
claude_notes: ZICO/DELS chunk confirms A: capacitated optimal policy has sequences with at most one partial-production period. Reviewer REVISE SA:3 too conservative.
codex_notes: A matches the definition of capacity-constrained production sequences exactly.

b: Planning systems generate demand forecasts and develop sourcing plans, whereas execution systems focus on producing goods to order and automatically replenishing stock based on actual orders.
c: Planning systems are responsible for managing the flow of products from suppliers to customers, while execution systems handle the coordination among internal processes and external business partners.
d: Planning systems enable firms to schedule production and monitor inventory levels, but execution systems do not have any role in these activities.

Supply chain management software is divided into two main categories: planning systems and execution systems. Which of the following statements accurately describes a key difference between these two types of systems?

a) Planning systems are used for modeling existing supply chains, while execution systems manage the flow of products through distribution centers.

b) Planning systems generate demand forecasts and develop sourcing plans, whereas execution systems focus on producing goods to order and automatically replenishing stock based on actual orders.

c) Planning systems are responsible for managing the flow of products from suppliers to customers, while execution systems handle the coordination among internal processes and external business partners.

d) Planning systems enable firms to schedule production and monitor inventory levels, but execution systems do not have any role in these activities.

a: The Zero Inventory Ordering (ZIO) policy in the uncapacitated DELS model ensures that an order is placed only if the inventory at the end of the previous period is zero, and it applies to both time-independent and time-dependent costs.
b: In the capacitated DELS model, the ZICO policy states that any optimal solution satisfies I_{t-1} · (C_t − y_t) · y_t = 0 for all t = 1, …, T. This means that in each period, at most one of the following can be strictly positive: the beginning inventory I_{t-1}, the unused capacity C_t − y_t, or the order quantity y_t itself.
c: The ZIO policy implies that each order placed in the optimal solution covers exactly the demands of several consecutive periods. This reduces the problem from choosing both order times and quantities to choosing only order times, significantly simplifying the optimization.
d: In the capacitated DELS model, if t is the last period in which production is positive (t = max{j: y_j > 0}), then the optimal production quantity in that period is y_t = min{C_t, d_t + … + d_T}, i.e., produce as much as possible up to the remaining demand or the capacity limit.
gen_text_clean:
question:
a) The Zero Inventory Ordering (ZIO) policy in the uncapacitated DELS model ensures that an order is placed only if the inventory at the end of the previous period is zero, and it applies to both time-independent and time-dependent costs.

b) In the capacitated DELS model, the ZICO policy states that any optimal solution satisfies I_{t-1} · (C_t − y_t) · y_t = 0 for all t = 1, …, T. This means that in each period, at most one of the following can be strictly positive: the beginning inventory I_{t-1}, the unused capacity C_t − y_t, or the order quantity y_t itself.

c) The ZIO policy implies that each order placed in the optimal solution covers exactly the demands of several consecutive periods. This reduces the problem from choosing both order times and quantities to choosing only order times, significantly simplifying the optimization.

d) In the capacitated DELS model, if t is the last period in which production is positive (t = max{j: y_j > 0}), then the optimal production quantity in that period is y_t = min{C_t, d_t + … + d_T}, i.e., produce as much as possible up to the remaining demand or the capacity limit.

correct_key: C
difficulty: hard
claude_notes: Null stem. Options a (ZIO: order when I=0 - TRUE), b (ZICO formula - TRUE), c (ZIO: covers consecutive demands - TRUE) are all correct. Multiple true options + null stem.
codex_notes: Missing stem makes the item malformed. Option C is a supported statement about ZIO implications, but the item should be revised into a proper single-question format.

question: In an EOQ model with planned backorders, if the shortage cost π increases while the holding cost h remains constant, how does this affect the optimal order quantity Q* and the minimum average cost C(s*,Q*)?
a: Both Q* and C(s*,Q*) increase.
b: Q* decreases but C(s*,Q*) increases.
c: Q* increases but C(s*,Q*) decreases.
d: Both Q* and C(s*,Q*) decrease.
gen_text_clean:
Question:
In an EOQ model with planned backorders, if the shortage cost π increases while the holding cost h remains constant, how does this affect the optimal order quantity Q* and the minimum average cost C(s*,Q*)?

a) Both Q* and C(s*,Q*) increase.
b) Q* decreases but C(s*,Q*) increases.
c) Q* increases but C(s*,Q*) decreases.
d) Both Q* and C(s*,Q*) decrease.

Correct Key: B

Difficulty: hard
claude_notes: EOQ with backorders, pi increases: Q*=sqrt(2KD/h)*sqrt((h+pi)/pi) DECREASES toward EOQ. C(s*,Q*)=sqrt(2KDh)*sqrt(pi/(h+pi)) INCREASES toward optimal EOQ cost. Key B (Q* decreases, C increases) is correct. No key marker. Fix key format.
codex_notes: As pi increases, Q* = sqrt(2KD/h)*sqrt((pi+h)/pi) decreases while C(s*,Q*) = sqrt(2KDh)*sqrt(pi/(pi+h)) increases, so keyed answer B is actually correct despite the reviewer note.
